CURRENT AFFAIRS,

it The Horse Show to most visitors to that function is
not a horse show, it is a man and woman show—to go
further it is a millinery show. The last Horse Show
was a really fine millinery turn out, and the tailors

| also did their part to make the scene effective. There
¢ were a very large number of gentlemen in the jumping
G enclosure on Wednesday, including such a distinguished
gentleman as the Inner Guard Sword Bearer of the
Secret Society by the name of the Free and Accepted
Masons of Ireland—Brother Goulding. Indeed we hap-
pened to find ourselves at one time standing some ten
yards from that distinguished “gentleman,”  The
usual * gaping . crowd of well-dressed mobsmen
peered up at the Viceregal box as if there were
lions and tigers there, and as if they, the on-
lookers, had been brought out by the hand to the Zoo.

- Yet withal it was hard to feel out of sympathy with
the gencral crowd there; they were a pleasant, well-
looking collection of people out for a holiday and on
arade in their Sunday best. There was plenty of pow-
ler and' that sort of thing, and the accents were often
about as genuine as some of the golden hair. Needless
to say innumerable Berties: were coming from Switzer-

i

i Ll

land; ladies who might cut one anothers throats only
that it was safer to cut one anothers characters, were
“so glad” and ‘“awfully delighted’’ to meet one
another, particularly since it was such a “ goodish long
time ’’ since they had beamed—consummate play actors
as they are—at one another. The very latest fashion in
the pronunciation of the word “ here '’ was worn largely
on the tongue. The suits of clothes lifted their hats
when the equivalent of “damn the Idolators” was
struck up as Dudley, accompanied by a fearless body of
soldiers—De Wet not being at the show—the suits of
clothes lifted their hats as Dudley went off with himself,
no doubt feeling relieved that part of the day’s work
was done. We were standing by at the time, and as
we kept our hats on we felt a pair of scornful eyes
peering at us; we turned and discovered an old lady

_giving a fierce “ damn the Idolators’’ look at us which

amused us. There were some feeble cheers as Dudley
passed outl. Devolution had probably taken all the gas
out of the bottles of loyalty on either side: they were
rather flat.

The “ unproductive classes’’ were well represented
at the Horse Show. We do not mean the brainless
types of humanity who largely frequent such functions.
We refer only to those whom Sir Horace Plunkett in a
vivid flash of insight, would style the “unproductive
classes.”” The “obscurantist’’ convents were conspicuous
in the Art and Industrial Section. The Providence tech-
nical woollen manufactory, which, as our readers know,
was founded by -a mere nun, and is successfully con-
ducted by the well-known religious order of the Sisters
of Charity, had as usual an exhibit. We understand
that the Foxford industry now gives employment to
150 hands, practically all of whom are natives of the
district. What a pity it is that some of the Foxford
Sisters could not be co-opted on the Council of Sir
Horace’s Department.

In Class 4 of the lace exhibits first prize was won by
St. Louis Convent, OCarrickmacross. First prizes in
this section also fell to the Convent of Poor Clares,
Kenmare; The Sisters of Charity, Benada Abbey, Co.
Sligo; Presentation Convent, Cashel; and Ardee Con-
vent, Louth. In Class 45 of stained glass work, first
prize we notice went to An Tiap Slome %4 Upper
Pembroke Street, Dublin.  The Sisters of Charity,
Ballaghaderin had a display of hosiery, etc., in the
Industrial Section.  Messrs. Hopkins and Hopkins,
O’Connell Bridge, had an exhibit of silver cups, medals,
and other examples of their manufacture. Horticul-
ture was represented by Messrs. A. Dickson and Sons,
and Messrs. C. Ramsay and Sons. The exhibits also
included one from the County Meath Home Industries
Association of various items of plain and fancy work,
the home work of girls in the County Meath.

A British “sporting '’ paper, by the name of The
Sporting Chronicle, lashed itself into a fine frenzy of
oloquent denunciation of an article which appeared in
our columns in our issue of August 19th. It was a °
very able, moderate and timely article—we mean the
article in the LEapER—and this assertion needs no fur-
ther proof than the spasms it gave our elite British con-
temporary 7'he Sporting Chronicle. The article that got
badly on the nerves of our betting tipster contemporary
was by our contributor * Achilles,’ and was entitled
“ Newspapers that incite to betting—a scruple.””

The British tipster paper appropriately headed its
foebly-strong diatribe, “In  the Silly Season.” There
" can be no doubt about it; our contemporary wrote not
only in the silly season but in thorough conformity with

b

" land.

»the spirit of that annual British period. We fear the
silly season in Britain will soon be commensurate with
all the year round.. The Sﬂli Season ” Editorial
writer of our contemporary, the bookies’ friend, evidently
looked over all our columns. We are glad to see that,
as our readers are wont to do, he also read the adver-
tisements.  He wrote “ Advertisments of celebrated
ciders confronted us.”” Power’s Irish Blackwater cider
made at Dungarvan, and Rutter’s cider made in Cork
city are certainly to be congratulated on the notice
taken of them by our British betting contemporary. If
the betting fraternity confined their beverages to
Power’s anid Rutter’s ciders it would benefit considerably
an honourable Irish industry, and no doubt the betting
fraternity might also feel none the worse of the change
after a while. We thank our betting contemporary for
its leading article on ourselves. ~ Our contributor
“Achilles,”” who provoked this silly season screech in the
“ bookies ”’ friend has an article in this issue also. We
commend it to our British sporting contemporary; we
hope the “Silly Season’’ Editor will read it; but we
almost feel like “laying odds’’ that it will completely
stagger him.

The Independent is blowing very hard about its circu-
lation in comparison with the circulation of the F'recman.
Now why confine itself to the Freeman, why does it not
shriek against the Zrish Times. For our part we would
be glad to see the Independent cutting into the Irish
Times, the libeller of Canon McInerney. Whilst the
Nationalists were fighting in the last decade of the old
century, the libeller of Canon McInerney was strength-
ening its position; and it is an anomalous thing that
the most prosperous-looking daily paper in the metro-
polis should be one that gloated over the proposed rob-
bing of twenty-two Parliamentary seats from Ireland.
Take a bite out of A/f’s calf, is our advice to the
Independent.

Pioneers of Irish industrial enterprise have enough to
contend with in the ordinary way, and it takes plenty
of pluck to found a new industry in Ireland. Messrs.
MacMenamin Bros. who recently started a factory for
-the manufacture of boot blacking, floor polish, etc., at
‘Edmonstown, Rathfarnham, have had to contend against
the effects of the recent torrents as well as the various
difficulties in the way of all Trish manufacturers in Tre-
The newly ecrected annexes to the factory were
swept away by the flood, the turbine was badly damaged,
and one of the partners of the firm, Mr. Brian Mac-
Menamin, was so badly injured that he had to be re-
moved to the Meath hospital to have his wounds
dressed. “Those of our readers who may be customers of
this firm will, we are sure, not only sympathise with
them in their unexpected and practically unforseeable
misfortune, but will extend to them in business matters
any consideration that may reasonably be called for.

We are*glad to note that some representative Ulster
Gaelic Leaguers are concerned ahout the work of con-
solidating the working units of the North. It would be
a good thing if we had an Ulster, a Leinster, and a
‘Connacht Council as well as a Munster Council. A meet-
ing is to be held at Derry with a view to creating a
permanent Council for the purpose of promoting the
sork of the Gaelic League in Ulster. These provincial
movements are movements in the right direction; no
people know their own business better than themselves.

Father Yorke, of San Fransisco, delivered an address
on the occasion of the celebration of the Golden Jubi-
lee of the Augustinian Church at Galway last Sunday.
Father Yorke is not by any means a “ tolerance prover.”’
In the course of his address he said:—*To-day the
evil genius of ascendancy still infests the country, and
you know by experience that it is still a real positive
disadvantage in every walk of life for a man to be a
Catholic in this Catholic land. To bigotry our churches
are a perpetual reminder that bigotry did its work and
failed. They are the assertion of that equality in
religion which our people won in name with such
tribulation, and they are perpetual incentives to make

THE LEADER.

SEpTEMBER 2, 1905.

that equality a fact not only in religion bub also in
cconon?ics, iz education, in social life, and in the poli-
tical field. We ask for nothing but what in all reason
is due to us—equality of rights and equality of oppor-
tunities.”

He also said:—“ We have a,ccomp!ighcd mxghb}:]l;
things against more powerful adversaries, and Wi 4
weapons not half as strong. You are Catholics an !
Trishmen. This is our country, and here should your
children dwell. If they arc to remain and prosper
they must have equal rights with the Chll‘dl‘en of ever.y
other class and creed. Bspecially in this age, where
more than in any other age the prizes of‘hfc go to the
educated must they have equal rights in school and
college and university.” Evidently _Fabhcr Yorke,
fresh from the Westorn States, appreciates the neces-
sity of the fight for Tolerance, Justice and Fair Play
for “Idolators’’ in Ireland.

The Cork Industrial Development Asst}cintioq have
decided to open the annual local industrial exhibition
on Monday, November 20th, and it will be continued
up to and including Saturday, December 2nd. During
the course of the exhibition, a conference of
representatives of all the Irish Industrial Develop-
ment Associations, the Irish County Councils, and other
public bodies, as well as prominent Irish public men,
will be held. The conference will meet in the Council
chamber on the two days following the opening day of
the exhibition. This conference should certainly ad-
vance the practical possibilities of the Industrial Move-
ment in so far as that movement can be advanced by
means of gencral organised effort.  Various localities
have taken action and have formed associations, and it
is time that representatives from the various associa-
tions should now confer. No doubt whilst in no way
hindering the freedom and utility of the various local
associations, it will probably be possible to decide some
general working plan by which the utility of each may
be increased by a rational union amongst the various
individual associations.

Even in Cork many of the distributing houses are
miles behind where they ought to be in this industrial
movement. A lady recently told Mr. E. J. Riordan,
the Secretary of the Cork association, of her difficulty
in getting good quality Trish knitted goods in Cork.
She stated she had visited one after another of the prin-
cipal shops in Cork, and though they almost bewildered
her with stocks of foreign-knitted goods, which they en-
deavoured to make her purchase, they were unable to
show her what she wanted of Irish manufacture.

The Council of the Cork Industrial Association, we
are not surprised to hear, considered this a scandalous
state of affairs, as it had been assured times without
number during the past three years by. various drapery
firms in Cork that they were stocking quantities of Irish
knitted goods, and yet with regard to no other anticle,
perhaps, had so many complaints been received from
persons wishing to purchase, but unable to do so, except
at a very few of the Cork houses. That Irish knitted
goods can compete with the world in quality, style,
finish and price is an established fact, and that they
should be boycotted by Irish drapery houses is a clear

indication of the depth of interest those firms take in

the encouragement of Irish industry.

Our revelations concerning the state of affairs re
the staffing of the Great Northern Railway commenced
in our issue of March 7th, 1903, about 2% years ago;
our revelations concerning the staffing of the Provincial
Bank appeared in our issue of January 30th, 1904,
going on for two years ago; yet practically nothing has
been done with a view to establishing open competitive
oxaminations in connection with the clerkships of these
two public institutions. ~ Half-yearly meebings have

come and gone and practically nothing has been done.
A mild protest or two was raised concerning the Great
Northern Railway Co., but even so much did not
happen at any meeting of the Provincial Bank. The

|

3
~ country has hiad jplenty time to:think over these matters
now, and ‘we suggest that it ds full time that .com-
mittees of :shareholdexs of these companies were formed
. in the nntex:e_sts of Justice, Tolerance .and .Fair Play.
The competitive examination system, with some gualifi-
cabions, was secured in connection with the Great
Southorn .and Western Railway, and in connection with
the Midland Railway .as a direct result of the actions of
the several committees of Catholic sharcholders. No
proper effort has yet been made in the Boardroom of
the Great Northern Railway 'to secure somo ¢hance of
tolerance, justice and fair play for the employees of
that company who are Papists, and for mere Papists
who, in the Aordumry way, ought ‘to stand s much
chance as their neighbours of obtaining. employment in
~ the service of that railway.

In the case of the Provincial Bank the action of
shareholders could 'be backed up by the support of cus-
N tomers. If you have to go to Belfast by rail from
| Dublin and many other places, you must go by the
{ "Great Northern line—there is no alternative; but in
the case of banks it is quite different; if you are not
pleased with one bank there are plenty of others to
choose from. About the time that our revelations con-
{ cerning the Provincial Bank appeared the directorate
| consisted of 10, 9 of whom were English and Scotch
i Protestants and one of whom was an Irish Papist. The
‘- general staff of five were all non-Catholic, and four of
1

them were Scotch as well. On the managerial staff 52

were Protestants and only 2 Catholics; of the accoun-

tants and tellers 75 were Protestants and 8 were Catho-
{ lics, and of the clerical staff 170 were Protestants and
1 19 were Papists. Now, considering the excellent
| secondary education that is supplied to Irish Catholic
boys by the various Catholic schools of the country,
{ why should not Catholic boys héave a fair chance, they
| want no more than a fair chance, of entering the ser-
vice of the Provincial Bank, the business of which is so
largely contributed by the Catholic public? We hope
shortly to hear that a shareholders’ committee has been
formed, and that at the next half-yearly meeting of
the bank that the directors will be asked to throw
- their clerkships open to some scheme of competitive
examination, such as now obtains, on the G. S. &
W. R., and that will offer some fair prospects to those
youths eligible and anxious to take service in the bank,
notwithstanding that they may have the economic dis-
advantage of being ‘‘ Idolators.”

Another Convent that is not afraid to put Irish
forward in its curriculum is Loreto Convent,

Navan. In our columns this week this Convent pub-
lishes the results of examinations. in musical
subjects which can speak for themselves. ~We

look forward with interest to the publication of this
year’s Intermediate results of our Irish schools.

The Sinn Femn Loan Fund and Investment Socicty
] 784 Rathmines Road, is now in full working order. We
14 badly needed a little “Sinn  Femn ”’ spirit in such
undertakings. There are no foreign speculators hiding
ander the Sinn Femn Society. Its organisers are all
Irishmen and we are glad to learn that their initiative
and enterprise are meeting with success.

Our funny confemporary the Zrish Protestant does not
appear to be a very prosperous property. It dragged
along for come timo and then an offort was made to
float it as a Limited Company. £1,478 4s. 0d. was
subscribed by way of capital, and during the trading
for the ‘year and nine months ending the last day o’f

, June, this well-managed organ of anti- “ obscurantism
and “light *’ succeeded in losing £1,183 0s. 7d. On the
 30th of June the cash in hands totalled up to the sum
" of one shilling and fourpence. The shareholdexs Eausl;
have folt ‘delighted when the{ wero told that the
directors expect that some further Ioss'wxlzl be entailed
before the Company can be self-supporting. We should
think so. We suppose that the chairman of (llll'ecb?rs
of this prosperous concern would threaten to resign like

 THE LEADER.

Brother Goulding, if a shareholders’ committee of in-
quiry were formed. Nothing succeeds like success, and
we feol sure that that 1s. 4d. in hand is the acorn oub
of which the oak tree of success will grow. C?apbam
Wado Thompson of the Pervert Priests Protection So-
ciety ought to lend a hand, and perhaps the Solicitor-
General would give the prosperous concern the benefit
of his advice, and mayhap Tyrell of the Parochial would
write a sonnet for it. ana is dead; but this other
“long-felt-want ’’ had 1s. 4d. cash in hand on June
30th. While there’s life there’s hope.

. We take the following advertisement from the I7islk
Times :— Country Protestant Boys, of good character,
ages 13 to 17 years, seeking positions in Dublin, may
receive help by applying to Superintendent, Working
Boys’ Home, 2 to 12 Lord Edward Street, Dublin.”
That is a simple and innocent-looking advertisement.
It is surely a laudable thing that some kind Protes-
tants in Dublin should band themselves together for
the purpose of helping lads of their own persuasion to
get on in life, and it were to be wished that kind
“Idolators ”” would show more concern in this direction
for the hosts of young “ Idolators”’ who are constantly
looking out for an opening in life.

We draw particulav attention to this advertisement,
however, for a specific reason. This Protestant Work-
ing Boys’ Home has a direct, if unofficial, connection
with the Great S. and W. Railway. The Hon. Sec.
of this Protestant Home is paid Secretary of the Rail-
way Company, the Chairman of which is Inner Guard
Sword Bearer of the secret plotting society by the name
of the Free and Accepted Masons of Ireland. With a
high official of the Freemasons in the chair, and the
Hon. Sec. of a society anxious to push on Protestant
boys, chief in the Secretary’s room, it is not to be won-
dered that we had occasion to style the concern the
Great Sourface Railway.

Now the Secretary of the G. S. and W. Railway,
Francis B. Ormsby, in his other capacity as Hon. Sec.
of this institution for, amongst other things, getting
young “Saved ’’ lads situations, issued a circular some
time about the close of 1903. In that circular this
high official of the G. S. and W. Railway, whose chair-
man is an eminent Freemason, said :— There appears
to be little doubt that a great organisation is now at
work in Dublin, with the object of ousting fellow-Pro-
testants from employment, and forcing employers of
labour to fill their places with Roman Catholics wunder
the cruel penalty of the ‘boycott.” The matter has
been referred to by more than one of our Bishops in
their recent charges, and need not be entered into more
fully here.” Thus two years ago did this man, this
Secretary of the G. S. and W. Railway, whose chair-
man is a high-placed Freemason, attempt to inflame the
already over-bearing animosity against Catholic Irish-
men. In view of these facts the innocent-looking ad-
veortisement in the Jrash Z'imes of Tuesday last is sig-
nificant.

The leading article in our contemporary the 7Zwans
Herald, of August 26th, is devoted to “ The Philosophy.
of Irish Ireland.”” The article commences:—* This
title of a work just issued by the Editor of the LeADER
suggests to us many sad reflections upon recent events
in this country and their general tendency. As Mr,
Moran, in this intevesting collection of very able essays
asks himself, we, too, are tempted to put to ourselves
some of the same pertinent and serious qustions, and
like him .are we forced to accept, unfortunately, the-
same inevitable conclwsions.” By the way, we may
say that there has been quite an wnexpected run on the
boolk, and uafertunately, our printers broke up the type
s0 that a second impression cannot be issued. The
edition. will probably be very shortly exhausted.

THE TRAGI-COMEDY.

R. O’BRIEN is about to resurrect his paper,
l the Irish People. He announces that it will
appear on Thursday, September 28th. We were sur-
prised at the sudden and quite dramatic disappearance
of that paper, and now after a long spell in the grave
it is about to come to life again; we almost hear it
mumbling— Boys of the Pool, here we are again.”
Mr. O’Brien does not love the Freeman’s Journal, and
it would appear that the Independent has a sort of
sneaking regard for William, if not for his own sake
at least for the sake of its grudge against the Freeman.
The new Irish People will, no doubt, be lively reading
for a time; but even so, it will have its sad side; it is
pitiful to have all this wrangling in face of the common
onemy entrenched behind their Orange ditches and
their Freemason signs.

There is not much hope for this country until honest
thought gets a fairer chance than it does. There is
too much timorous ‘“standing in’’ with the clique or
party on top at the moment. Men who shouted
blindly for Mr. William O’Brien when that gentleman
was up are now, no doubt, ready to shout as blindly
against him when he is down. We are more or less
impartial spectators to the act of the sad tragi-comedy
that is now being unfolded.

Five years ago Mr. O’Brien thought all would be
plain sailing if only the truculent Tim Healy were
bundled away to political oblivion; to-day many shal-
low men aro thinking that all would be well if only
cxcitable William would have kept being ‘ resigned ;’’
when will Irishmen léarn that things cannot be well
until they consent to face issues on their merits and
like nien, and cease “ standing in ”’ with any temporary
strong party that may, at any given time, be on top?

Mr. O’Brien’s paper is to appear on September-28.
In our issue dated September 29, 1900, we had some-
thing to say about the famous cock-fight between Mr.
O’Brien and Mr. Healy at Louth. O’Brien at that
timo thought all would be well if Healy were wiped
out; many to-day are thinking that all would be well
if O’Brien remained “resigned.”’ All the while the
great forces that make or kill nations worked on like
the mills of the gods; if O’Brien remained resigned the
people who will not open their eyes to the living issues
that involve the life of nations, would, one by one,
have occasion to feel that all would be well if Tom,
Dick, Harry, would only keep “shut up.” It all
arises from ~ want of masculine strength and broad,
straight thinking, the want of the will or the power to

K PROJECTED IRISH. ACADEMY.

7T HE last Ard-Fheis of the Gaelic Leaguo adopted a

r scheme of Irish education put before them by
the Coisto Gnotha of the same body. In this scheme a
ssyllabus is to be drawn up comprising-4 or b successive
@oxaminations, the final examination being of such a

2 THE LEADER.

SEPTEMBER. 2, 1905:

character as to entitle the student who succeeds in
passing it to a place in a projected Irish Academy.
The creation of this Academy is the only part of this
scheme which is novel. It is proposed to elect its first
members without examination. The scheme proposes
that about 12 or 15 of the greatest living Irish scholars
be elected as the nucleus of an Irish Academy (which
shall act as an examining Board); that thenceforth
admission to the ranks of the Academy be by examina-
tion. It is proposed morcover to leave the election of
the initial members to the present Coiste Gnotha. A
small sum it is said, has been voted by the Ard-Fheis out
of the funds of the Gaelic League to meet the expenses
of the scheme during the current year.

In considering this scheme it is well to bear in mind
that within the last few years two other examination
schemes slightly less ambitious than the present, were
passed by the Ard-Fheis, and that an attempt was made
to work them by the Education Committee of the Coiste
Gnotha. The attempt, however, in both cases was not
very successful. An examination scheme embracing the
entire country is a serious matter and cannot be suc-
cessfully handled without a suitable staff entirely de-
voted to it and sufficient funds. Apart altogether from
any intrinsic defects these schemes may have had their
failure was inevitable from want of suitable machinery
and from want of money. It is with the fullest ad-
vertance to the failure of the previous schemes that
this new and ambitious scheme culminating in an Irish
Academy is launched. It is at last recognised that such
a scheme requires a special staff, and that money must
be provided. Whether the provision made by the Ard-
Theis for machinery and money will prove sufficient to
insure the success of the scheme remains to be seen. It
is, however difficult to see how any scheme of the kind
worthy of serious consideration can work even for a year
on the small sum provided for it. Everybody who has
watched the development, of Irish studies and the growth
of the language movement, must feel that there is a great
need for some system of Irish studies proceeding by pro-
gressive stages from an eclementary course to a course
suitable for advanced students. Lovers of the language
will not fail to be in sympathy with the projected scheme
in so far as it provides examinations proceeding on pro-
gressive lines. But the circumstances under which it
is launched forbid one to be very sanguine of its success.
Any scheme of its kind depending on voluntary contri-
butions for its support must ever be in imminent dan-
ger of failing. It is the duty of the Government to
institute and endow a scheme of this kind, but at pre-
sent there seems little hope of their performing this
duty. The scheme in question cannot work if sufficient
endowment: be not forthcoming, and if its administration
be not placed at the outset in competent hands. The
money difficulty is far from being the only dificulty it
is sure to encounter. A serious difficulty will arise in
the constitution and powers of the proposed Academy.

It seems to the present writer that the time is not
ripe for an institution deserving the name of an Aca-
demy or which could command the respect and intel-
lectual submission of Irish students. Irish studies have
been so hampered, so circumseribed, so partial, so de-
sultory, that but little solid result can as yet be pointed
to. So much still remains to be done, not only in ex-
ploring and publishing Irish. literature from existing
manuscripts, but also in catching up and recording every
faintest whisper of the living speech ere it dies on the
lips of the out-going generation. The hope of Irish
studies lies in the young genevation of workers who are
familiar with the living speech, and. who have been
taught to sot store on our literary remains as well as
on a painstaking accuracy in handling them. To set up
an Acndqmy now in which certain scholars are to got a
place, without examination, solely by virtue of the
work they have given to the world, appears to me like
plucking unripe apples. I am. far indeed from desiring
to be-little the work that has been done, but T regard

it as too partial and imperfect to warrant for its authors
the precise recognition that an Academy worthy of the
name would' give. ;

;Now as to the personnel of the Academy. It is ob-
vious that Modern Irish—spoken and written—is tho
study which it is expected to cultivate, and proficiency
in which must be the title of its members to distinction,
I would consider, therefore, that a member of such a
body elected solely in virtue of his past work, and with-
out examination, should be a fluent and accurate speaker
of living Irish, one whose hold of the spoken language
no one dare call in question, that he should bo a man of
general learning versed in the classics and in somo of
the modern languages, having, if possible, academic
distinctions. That he should have done work of un-
doubted scholarship in Ivish; work, too, of an important
character, and lastly that his name be well and widely
known as an Irish scholar and a successful worker in the
fields of Irish literature. To these qualifications I
should like to add another, namely : that he should not
merely be a scholar but a man of undoubted literary
power in Irish. Nobody who takes the trouble to think
the question out will be likely to quarrel with these
qualifications, and it is obvious that a strict insistence
on them in every Academician is of the utmost impor-
tance, as, no matter how favourable other circumstances
may be to the success of the enterprise, it is certain to
be a disastrous failure if the initial members, the very
corner-stone of the building, are not selected according
to the principles universally recognised as governing
institutions of the kind.

A member of such an institution should be a man of
such distinction that there will be no necessity to ask
who he is, he should be a man of undoubted learning
and culture, and one who has wrestled successfully with
some of the difficulties That lie in the path of the Irish
scholar. No mere speaker of Irish, no matter how
fluent, no mere writer of even good idiomatic Irish if
devoid of the other qualifications mentioned would de-
serve a place in such an institution. The advanced
student who speaks Irish fairly well but with a halt
and a lisp, who writes Inrish as best he may, stilted,
cramped, rheumatic Irish, would scarcely find himself
comfortable in such an institution. There are of course
a few men of acknowledged scholarship and good edu-
cation who are not fluent speakers of the language and
who are unable to write living Irish with freedom.
Now, are there twelve or fifteen Irish scholars who
comply even in a moderate way with the qualifications
I have sketched above? I fear not. The number of
such persons to be found is less than twelve. The
Coiste Gnotha can hardly be envied for the task set to
them of making the selection in question.

A Board consisting largely of “advanced students’’
would, to my mind, be worse than useless, it would be
calculated to set up a false standard of scholarship, and
would put back the hands of the clock of Irish scholax-
ship a full generation. It is scarcely necessary to say
that a person who has made a reputation for the
vigour and success of his propagandist work if he be not
at the same time a scholar, would not add to the
authority or dignity of the new Academy. Then there
is the provincial difficulty. Other things being equal
it would be desirable to have every province in Ireland
represented on the Academy. But other things should
be'equal. The principle of electing a man, not becauso
he is a scholar, a man of undoubted learning and
literary ability, a man widely known and universally
recognised as an authority, not because he has these
qualities but because he represents a particular province,
such & principle is calculated to reduce the power and
prestige of the entire body to almost the vanishing
point. If I may offer a suggestion to the Coisto
Gnotha, I would advise the election by that body of
half a' dozen or so men about whom there can be no
question, and’ throw on that half dozen the onus of
co-opting the remaining six or nine.

I was stationary long enough,’” said a country de-
legate to me. I agreed. A stationary movement
sounded a little contradictory ; but then we are full of
contradictions, and when one comes to think of it we
certainly have a good ‘many “stationary movements
in Ircland.

This movement, however, is undoubtedly progressing.
Last week’s Town Tenants’ Convention was a distinct
advance upon the first convention held twelve months
ago.. It was larger, more representative and more in-
toresting. As to what work it did, a gathering of the
kind is not to be judged by its agenda paper. It serves
a more useful purpose than that of merely passing a
number of resolutions. Coming once a year, it has
its usefulness for a cause and a movement, just as a
holiday has its usefulness for a man and his business.

Iu our Shop of State—not Ship of State, Mr. Comp.,
please—the Land Question long occupied the most pro-
minent place in the front window. It hasn’t dis-
appeared yet, but it doesn’t take up nearly so much
room as it used to. It was not, however, to be ex-
pected that the newly available space would be allowed
to remain empty. So long as the shop stands, and no
matter what changes come about, the windows will al-
ways be full of grievances, more or less genuine, moce
or less important. Now, there appears to be a dis-
position to fill the vacant space with the Town Tenants’
Question. That will never do. It would be bad busi-
ness, bad policy. It would lead to a miserable failure,
and only make the place resemble a huxter’s shop.
There is plenty of room in the window for the Town
Tenants’ Question, which has been too long left lying
by ‘““on the shelf ’’ inside, but it never will fill the
space once occupied by the Land Question. What is
wanted is, not an attempt to make too much of it, but
to attract people to support it by making them thor-
oughly understand it and the solutions proposed.

At the present time the Town Tenants’ Question is
not half understood by the public, or even by the
tenants themselves. The tenants, of course, know a
good deal about their grievances, but they are very hazy
about the remedies and how they are to be secured.
Agitation is but a cheerless prospect. They have seen
so much of agitation and got so little from it. Their
position is so different from that of the farmers.
Crowded together as they are in village, town and city,
there is yet an isolation about their situation unknown
on.the poorest agricultural estate. ~ With many in-
terests in common, which ought to lead to powerful
combination, selfishness, mistaken for self-interest,
keeps them apart. It is every man for himself, as on a
sinking ship, from the earth-owner, the lease-halder,
the middle-man, the shopkeeper, down to the dweller
in the depths. Agitation? Reform? All are at once
suspicious. Reform for one class means an increase in
the burdens of us others. Let them talk of their re-
form. We want none of it! It is hard enough to live
as it is. What stake have they in the place, that are
talking? And, then, look at the rates!

It is a difficult and complex problem. But it can be
made plain and easy to understand, and people must be
made to understand it before they will come to support
it. ‘“ Remember Mitchelstown!’’ has a meaning for
us all; when will ¢ Remember Kingstown!’’ come to
have ono? What happened in Kingstown ? Kingstown
knows; but how many other towns know?  And yet
there must be more than one Kingstown in Ireland,
where eoven without legislation such another injustice
could not be perpetrated if the people only showed a
spark of manhood. If they have not shown it, that is
not reason to despair. They have got it. They are
sound at heart. It is that they do not see, that they
do not understand. Iope can be infused into them,
as it was into the farmers. The Convention of last
week was a hopeful sign. There is a mission before
the Town Tenants’ League if it but read it aright.

7Y HERE is a legend that Doctors of Divinity were

A - at one time so common amongst the clergy in
some of the churches of Dublin, that a parish priest
with whom a bishop was staying for some time had to
warn the prelate—“ Don’t call yourself Doctor—or you
will be mistaken for one of my curates.”” Still if you
look at the list of Dublin priests in Thom’s Directory,
say, of 1880, you will find but a few D.D.’s and scarcely
a trace of B.A.’s. On the contrary, if you inspect the
names on the Archdiocese of Dublin for 1905 you will
see that the majority of the younger curates—pro-
bably three-fourths of them—are degree men.

Canon' Shechan gives us a delightful sketch of the
bewilderment of the old parish priest when his curate
of the new school sent up his visiting card, *“ Rev. Luke
Delmege, B.A., C.C.,”” and contrasts the hard-featured,
bicycle-riding, coffee-drinking cleric of the nower gene-
ration with the good-humoured, broad-featured, easy-
mannered priest of a previous time. I hope that Father
Sheehan will find leisure some day to draw us an out-
line of the psychology of the Dublin ecclesiastic, for he
-is distinctly different from his country colleague. As a
factor in the country’s progress he has scarcely yet
found his place, but his potentiality for its betterment
is enormous.

Those of the priests who are Royal University men
are almost a class apart. It has been denied
vigorously that the R.U.I. can make any mark in the
character-of those wha pass.-through its examination
balls, but the facts are all against this view, as the
sympathies, the thoughts, the prejudices and the con-
victions of almost all Catholic Royal University men
are marvellously alike. The predominating note in
the character of these ecclesiastical graduates is criti-
cism—not a harsh or corrosive criticism, but a disposi-
tion to assay or analyse the qualities of every man and
every movement, and not to be led merely by a blind
enthusiasm. They are essential hard workers, and
have almost as much faith as Carlyle in the regenerating
influence of labour. Somebody complains to me that
they are temperance men to an irritating extent—that
they belong to that class whom Dick Phenyl, in ‘‘Sweet
Lavender,”” sympathised with when he said—‘‘ I pity
the man who has never seen the world except through
the gloomy spectacles of sobriety.”’ Their attitude to-
wards most questions is that crystallised in the earlier
issues of the LEADER, but some are now critics even of
it.
~ The conversazione of the Royal University used to be
one of the few social events at which Irish priests and
laymen, Jesuits and Orangemen, Protestant bishops,
learned ladies and simple seminarists met in friendly
converse for at least one glorious night in the year.
Budding graduates looked forward with eager glee to
the time when they would participate therein. Last
year it was to have been revived, but a chilling frost
come over the promoters.

A certain incompatibility of ideals, interfered with
harmony of action. Some young ladies from the
Alexandra College thought that it would be pleasant to
bask in the smiles of Vice-royalty and the military, and
proposed to ask the Lord Licutenant. The Irish Ire-
land end of the committce, on the other hand, was
horrified at the prospect of taking part in a function
where ““ God save the King ’’ should be played, and the
result was that no conversazione was held in 1904.

We were promised that arrangements would com-
mence bright and early this year, and that the Gaels
would commence their preparations before the summer,
to show how much a festivity would be managed in &
Gaclic University. One hears nothing of the doings of
the conversazione committee, and it is almost time to
commence if we are to be in time for Degree Day,
October 28th. :

University graduate. In fact, a certain Dublin organ

The Certificate then gives the pious uses or trusts,
which are the same as those given in the 1st and 2nd -
Certificates with one exception. The two first Certifi-
cates direct that, if the £50 per annum, to be given to
5 English schools, should not be employed, the money

annum is to be devoted to the five English schools,

Smith shall, by his last will and testiment or other
writing sealed, appoint, and that in default of such ap-
pointment the said £50 per annum shall be employed
to the other general uses hereby intended.”” This pro-
vision taken in connection with Erasmus Smith’s sub-
sequent conduct throws a search-light on his intentions.
It shows that Erasmus Smith used the purpose of found-
ing 5 English schools as a plea to get lands, and that
when he had got the lands, he did not provide the
schools. For the Charter—the last instrument of foun-
dation—makes no provision for English schools, and
the letter of the Trustees to Erasmus Smith, dated

Hospital, London, those £50 per annum and the money
that ought to be devoted to clothing and apprenticing-
poor scholars, and to Exhibitions in Trinity College.
He made no special provision for Exhibitions in Trinity
College in the Charter.

The third and last Instrument of foundation is the
Charter of Charles II., dated the 26th of March, 1669.
It says:— Whereas Erasmus Smith, Esq., did hereto-
fore intend to erect.5 Grammar Schools within the

And whereas there was a Bill certified and trans-

cntitled “ An Act for Settling of Certain Lands of

tenements, etc., ought to be applied to the said charit-

And, whereas, in pursuance of two Acts of FParlia-

clouds then. A dog comes in as at a side door, and
barks. Shirr-r! From crag to crag goats and sheep;
stones tumbling down; wild birds skidding; and in a
moment rock is bare. I come out, and there is the
white, live torrent down the mountain, the dog and his
master on a crag above me.

On way down heard someone singing. Looked. Old
man and youth stretched in heather. Old man sing-

and humour—that there is a humour so-
‘called which is merely ridicule, and a humour truly-
called which is sympathy. The story of Billy Baxter is, T

- should hope, a record of sympathy. I loved Billy Baxter

(for he is real to me) and I could not have written his
story had I not loved him, and loved him much. I
think;, too, T am not alone in my love of him, If I may
f(fr a moment lapse into boastfulness, T should say that
!mxe.people out of ten who read his story arose from
it _thh love of honest, rough, bluff, big-hearted Billy
ﬁlI.m.g their hearts.  There are some friends whose
opinions carry full as much weight to the world as Mr.
Louis J. Walsh’s, and who are all, T venture to thinlk,
as fond of their country and their people as is M.
Walsh, who love Billy Baxter and his story more than
any character and any story that I have yet set down.
The words of one of these whom I last met at the Ard-
Fheis are “ Ah, you’ll never write anything to beat
Billy Baxter!” I do not recall any story of mine,
strange to say, that won for me more friends than did
this one of Billy.

Mr. Walsh was not particularly happy in selecting a
specimen of my “low travesty,” “loathsome stage
Irishmanism,”” and “buffoonery.”” The particular
loathsomeness of my picture of Billy, it seems, is that
I made him carry with him into his nephew’s grand
house in New York the homely manners that were his
in Ireland. By way of contrast between the freedom
of Ireland and the stiff formality of America (as per-
sonified in Margaret MacArthur), I made Billy present
himself at his nephew’s dinner-table in the same man-
ner in which, on a Sunday afternoon, he would sit
down to his own dinner-table—that is in the pride of
fresh and white shirt sleeves; when he saw his nephew’s
American wife treating the guests to preliminary courses
of tit-bits, he warned his neighbour in a loud whisper
to “ dail lightly ; take my word for it, and only take of
these what’ll fill the far lands,”’ for there was more, and
more substantial, to come. Outraging the very proper
feelings’ of the frigid American wife”of his nephew,
Billy would, in his white shirt sleeves, of which he was
justly proud, go out upon the front steps, and, in his
fine old-country way, persist in saluting every stranger
who passed and engaging willing ones in conversation
about the weather and the crops; also, finding that the
benighted youths of America knew not any grand
games, he gathered them around him and to the horror
of the street taught them the noisiest and most rollick-
ing Irish games he could think of. The proprieties of
America puzzled and harried poor Billy till, nigh break-
ing his heart, they drove him to his loved home again.
Mr. Walsh will be alarmed to know that of the creation
of such a loathsome bit of stage Irishmanism I should
like to be guilty about (not to be too greedy) twice a
week.

I well remember how, even as a child, the ruling
passion—for it was a passion—with me was love of Ire-
land and of our people. It was a consuming passion
with me. =~ I read of little else, I talked of little else.
I think I have not changed much. My love for our
people to-day is intense; I hope my love of country is
s0 likewise. I think there are not many people in Ire-
land whose love for our people exceeds mine—I love
their thousand lovable qualities of heart and soul—I
love their very foibles with a caressing love which I
should hope expresses itself in my poor attempts at
stories. I pen no line that is not inspired by loving

e sympathy. "I have a strong hatred for those who ridi-

cule our people intentionally. I have pity for the well-
meaning idiots who, not being able to discern between
humour and buffoonery, between sympathy and ridicule,
would bri idicule upon our people indirectly and
‘unintentionally.—Yours, etc.,

Seumas MacMANus.
